The most important thing to this type is that they've obtained all the information possible to make a decision. To the analyst, not reaching an agreement is simply another possible outcome. If they feel they've gotten all the information they can, they're happy to not make a deal. These types are methodical, diligent, pragmatic, and skeptical in nature. Time is of little consequence. As long as they believe they are working towards the best result in a thorough and systematic way, they will take as much time as they need to feel comfortable about their decision. The perception of themselves is linked to minimizing mistakes. Their models include, as much time as it takes to get it right, and I don't get mad, I get even. The classic analyst type prefers to work alone. They're very focused individuals. When speaking, they tend to hide their emotions and use the late night FM DJ voice we covered in the earlier courses. However, they often speak in a way that makes them come across as being distant and cold rather than soothing. Even if they don't realize it, analysts distance themselves from people, which limits their ability to put their counterpart at ease, and thus gather information. They hate getting caught off guard. To avoid this, they will research for weeks to get data they might have gotten in 15 minutes at the negotiation table. Keep in mind that they are reserved problem solvers and information collectors who are hyper-sensitive to reciprocity. They see it as a trap. They would never give something to you unless they were setting you up to give them something. So why wouldn't you do the same? The fact that they are talking to you at all is a concession. Inversely, each time you try to reach out to them, they will view it as a new piece of information that they need to evaluate. Expect them to take their time coming up with counter-proposals, making decisions, or answering significant questions. Build in a day or two for each significant response you want and the process will become much more predictable. Once they are skeptical in nature, questions raise their guard rather than lower it. They will want to think through all the implications of any answer before they give it, which will not only take a minute, but the answer will intentionally be limited. When dealing with these types, if they think you're unprepared, they'll lose respect for you and be happy to ambush you. Since they hate surprises, it's best to warn them of potential issues and problems early on so that you can preemptively reduce the shock. If you have to disagree with them, never say you disagree. Say, let's make a dispassionate comparison of data. They're happy to compare facts and data. They've worked so hard on it prior to coming to the table, they want to show it off. They want you to be happy to show yours off as well. They hate arguing. This type would rather have a conversation than a negotiation. Don't be concerned if they get quiet. They use the silence to think. They're not mad at you and they're not trying to give you a chance to talk more. Let them have their moment to think. Apologies have little value to them because they view the negotiation and your personal relationship largely as separate things. Since questions of any type put them on their guard, even calibrated ones, the best tools to use with them are labels. As a great opener with an analyst, it seems like you've put a lot of time into this. If you find that this description defines your style, be wary of cutting yourself off from an essential source of data, which is your counterpart. The best tactic to use to avoid being seen as cold and emotionless is to smile when you speak. People will be more forthcoming with information. Smiling is a simple habit to develop and it makes it easy for you to mask your emotions in a positive way, even if you get caught off guard. There are three strengths of the analyst. Your inclination to prepare, your voice, and your use of silence. You want these skills, but simultaneously need less of them. Number one, your inclination to prepare. You need less of it, probably about 60% less. You need to get your thinking to the table sooner and apply the desire for discovery there. Your thirst for information is a remarkable attribute that gives you a great advantage. Number two, your voice. Your natural downward inflection turns down all emotions. You need to use it only to turn down the negative ones. Blend in frequent doses of the smile and friendliness of the accommodator and your voice will be a powerful asset. Number three, set expectations for silence. You will find dealing with the other two types in these moments difficult when you're only looking for time to think. Click on one of the other options to learn more about the other negotiation types.
